title: MAP/Barometric Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
shortDescription: The MAP sensor signal is out of the expected range compared to other engine data.
fullDescription:
P0106 indicates the Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor is reporting a value inconsistent with engine operating conditions such as throttle position, RPM, and MAF readings. The ECM uses MAP data for fuel trim and timing calculations.

Common triggers include a vacuum leak, clogged MAP sensor port, or a sensor that has drifted out of calibration. Driveability symptoms are often subtle but can include poor fuel economy and hesitation.
safetyImpact: Generally low — driveability issues may cause hesitation at inopportune moments.

causes.cause: Vacuum leak in intake manifold or hose
Clogged or disconnected MAP vacuum port
Damaged wiring or poor connector contact

fixSteps.title: Scan and freeze-frame
fixSteps.instruction: Pull freeze frame data to see conditions when fault set.

fixSteps.title: Inspect vacuum hoses
fixSteps.instruction: Check for cracked, loose or disconnected vacuum lines.

fixSteps.title: Test MAP sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure signal voltage at idle and WOT against spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace MAP sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new OE-spec MAP sensor if readings are out of range.

faq.a: Short distances yes, but fuel trim errors can damage the catalyst over time.

faq.q: Is cleaning the MAP sensor worth trying?
faq.a: Yes — a quick spray with MAF/electronics cleaner can restore accuracy.

title: MAP/Barometric Pressure Circuit Low Input
shortDescription: The MAP sensor circuit voltage is below the expected minimum.
fullDescription:
P0107 is set when the ECM detects the MAP sensor signal voltage at or near 0 V for longer than the diagnostic threshold. This usually points to an open circuit, short to ground, or a failed sensor.

Because MAP is a primary fueling input on speed-density systems, this code typically causes noticeable driveability problems and may trigger limp mode.
safetyImpact: Limp mode may reduce power; engine may stall at idle.

causes.cause: Open 5 V reference supply to sensor

fixSteps.title: Check connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect MAP connector for bent, pushed out or corroded terminals.

fixSteps.title: Verify 5 V reference
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe reference wire — must read close to 5 V key on.

fixSteps.title: Check signal wire
fixSteps.instruction: Test for short to ground between sensor and ECM.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If wiring is good, replace the MAP sensor.

faq.q: Does a disconnected MAP set P0107?
faq.a: Yes — an unplugged sensor reads 0 V and will set this code immediately.

title: MAP/Barometric Pressure Circuit High Input
shortDescription: The MAP sensor signal voltage is above the expected maximum.
fullDescription:
P0108 is set when the MAP signal stays near 5 V, which corresponds to impossibly high manifold pressure. Typical causes are a short to voltage, failed sensor, or open ground.

The engine will usually run rich because the ECM assumes heavy load. Expect rough running and fouled spark plugs if left unrepaired.
safetyImpact: Rich mixture can foul plugs and damage the catalyst.

causes.cause: Signal wire shorted to 5 V or 12 V

fixSteps.title: Inspect connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check for water, corrosion, or damaged terminals.

fixSteps.title: Verify ground
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe ground wire — must show <0.1 V drop.

fixSteps.title: Test signal
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug sensor; signal wire at harness should drop to 0 V.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new MAP sensor and clear code.

faq.q: Can oil in the MAP sensor cause P0108?
faq.a: Yes — contaminated sensors often drift high. Replace rather than clean.

title: Intake Air Temperature Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: The IAT sensor reading is implausible for the engine operating conditions.
fullDescription:
P0111 is set when the Intake Air Temperature sensor value does not correlate with coolant temperature at cold start, or does not change appropriately as the engine runs.

The ECM uses IAT to calculate air density for fueling; a drifted sensor can cause lean or rich mixtures. Often caused by a sensor that has aged or been contaminated with oil.

fixSteps.title: Compare IAT vs ECT at cold start
fixSteps.instruction: Both should be within a few degrees after overnight soak.

fixSteps.title: Clean sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean with MAF cleaner if oil contaminated.

fixSteps.title: Replace IAT
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new IAT sensor if cleaning doesn't resolve.

faq.q: Is the IAT built into the MAF?
faq.a: On many vehicles yes — replacing the MAF assembly may be required.

title: Intake Air Temperature Circuit Low Input
shortDescription: The IAT sensor circuit voltage is below the normal operating range.
fullDescription:
P0112 indicates the IAT signal is at or near 0 V, corresponding to an impossibly hot intake charge. The ECM substitutes a default value, but fueling accuracy suffers.

Most commonly caused by a signal wire shorted to ground, a failed sensor, or connector corrosion.

causes.cause: Short to ground in IAT signal wire

symptoms: Slight hesitation or rough running when cold

fixSteps.title: Unplug sensor and read voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With sensor disconnected, signal wire should read ~5 V.

fixSteps.title: Check for short to ground
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance between signal wire and ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace IAT
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor if wiring tests good.

faq.a: Rarely — the ECM uses a default value and the engine usually runs.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Post-catalyst or third O2 sensor on Bank 1 shows a circuit fault.
fullDescription:
P0142 applies to vehicles with three oxygen sensors per bank. It indicates an electrical fault or implausible signal on Bank 1 Sensor 3 — typically located after a second catalyst or in a tailpipe position.

Causes are similar to other O2 codes: failed sensor element, damaged wiring, or contaminated sensor from coolant or oil consumption.

fixSteps.title: Scan all O2 data
fixSteps.instruction: Compare Bank 1 Sensor 3 output vs Sensors 1 and 2.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check for melted insulation where harness routes near exhaust.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor using anti-seize on threads.

faq.a: Denso/NTK/Bosch OE-equivalent sensors are usually fine; avoid unbranded units.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: The third O2 sensor on Bank 1 reports persistent low voltage.
fullDescription:
P0143 is set when the Bank 1 Sensor 3 signal stays at or below ~0.1 V for longer than the diagnostic window. This may indicate a lean exhaust condition, a shorted sensor, or an open signal circuit.

Inspect for exhaust leaks upstream before replacing the sensor — leaks draw in atmospheric air and cause false lean readings.

fixSteps.title: Inspect exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Look for leaks at flanges and gaskets upstream of sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check signal wire
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe signal; should follow commanded A/F.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor if wiring and exhaust check out.

faq.a: No — P0143 is almost always an electrical or exhaust-leak issue.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: The Bank 1 Sensor 3 O2 signal is stuck at high voltage (rich).
fullDescription:
P0144 indicates the third O2 sensor on Bank 1 reports voltages above roughly 0.9 V for longer than allowed. This typically points to a rich exhaust, contaminated sensor, or signal wire shorted to a 12 V source.

Common root causes include leaking injectors, high fuel pressure, and engine oil consumption coating the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check fuel trims
fixSteps.instruction: Long term fuel trim well negative confirms rich condition.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for oil burning
fixSteps.instruction: Blue smoke and oil consumption points to sensor contamination.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If root cause is addressed, replace the contaminated sensor.

faq.q: Why does my car burn more fuel?
faq.a: A stuck-rich reading causes the ECM to over-fuel, hurting economy.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: The Bank 1 Sensor 3 O2 sensor reacts too slowly to mixture changes.
fullDescription:
P0145 is set when the ECM commands a rich/lean transition but the O2 sensor takes too long to respond. An aged sensor element, silicone or lead contamination, and wiring resistance all contribute.

This is typically an age-related failure; sensors normally last 100k–150k miles.

fixSteps.title: Graph sensor output
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool to plot voltage during throttle snap.

fixSteps.title: Inspect connector
fixSteps.instruction: Clean corrosion and check pin tension.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor with anti-seize on threads.

faq.q: How long do O2 sensors last?
faq.a: Modern planar sensors typically 100k–150k miles, less if contaminated.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: No detectable switching activity from the Bank 1 Sensor 3 O2 sensor.
fullDescription:
P0146 indicates the third oxygen sensor on Bank 1 is reporting a flat signal near 0.45 V bias voltage, suggesting the sensor is not producing a response. Common causes include a failed sensor heater, disconnected harness, or a dead sensor.

Check heater circuit fuses and operation before condemning the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check heater fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the O2 heater fuse is intact.

fixSteps.title: Measure signal at ECM
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe ECM connector and verify wiring continuity.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor if wiring is intact.

faq.a: No — readiness monitors will not complete with this code active.

title: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Fault in the heater circuit for Bank 1 Sensor 3 O2 sensor.
fullDescription:
P0147 indicates the ECM has detected an electrical problem in the heater element circuit of the third oxygen sensor on Bank 1. Heaters bring the sensor to operating temperature quickly, allowing closed-loop fueling sooner after a cold start.

Causes include a blown heater fuse, open heater element, or harness damage.

fixSteps.title: Check fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm heater fuse is good.

fixSteps.title: Measure heater resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Typical value 4–15 ohms; open reads infinite.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor if heater is open.

faq.a: Yes, but expect an emissions failure and slightly higher fuel use.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
shortDescription: Electrical or signal fault on the downstream O2 sensor, Bank 2.
fullDescription:
P0156 is set when the ECM detects an out-of-range or invalid signal from the post-catalyst oxygen sensor on Bank 2. Since this sensor's main role is catalyst monitoring, the code is emissions-related rather than driveability-related.

Typical causes include a failed sensor, damaged wiring near exhaust heat, or an exhaust leak near the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Inspect sensor and harness
fixSteps.instruction: Look for melted or abraded wiring.

fixSteps.title: Check signal voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Compare downstream voltage to upstream during cruise.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install OE-quality replacement.

faq.q: Is P0156 the same sensor as P0136?
faq.a: No — P0156 is on Bank 2, P0136 is on Bank 1.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
shortDescription: Downstream O2 sensor on Bank 2 reports low voltage.
fullDescription:
P0157 indicates the Bank 2 downstream oxygen sensor voltage is stuck low, corresponding to a lean or no-signal condition. Exhaust leaks upstream of the sensor, a shorted signal wire, or a failed sensor are typical causes.

Inspect the exhaust for leaks before replacing the sensor, particularly at catalyst flanges and slip joints.

fixSteps.title: Inspect exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check flanges and catalyst for leaks.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Measure signal wire for short to ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor.

faq.q: Can a bad upstream O2 set P0157?
faq.a: Indirectly — lean running caused by upstream issues can trigger downstream codes.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
shortDescription: Downstream O2 sensor on Bank 2 reports persistently high voltage.
fullDescription:
P0158 is set when the Bank 2 post-catalyst O2 sensor voltage stays above ~0.9 V. Causes range from a contaminated sensor to a signal shorted to a voltage source or genuine rich running.

Fuel leaks, stuck injectors, and high fuel pressure can all cause this code, so verify fuel trims before replacing the sensor.
safetyImpact: Low — rich running may eventually damage the catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Verify fuel trims
fixSteps.instruction: Scan tool — negative long-term trims confirm rich.

fixSteps.title: Check for rich root cause
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect injectors, fuel pressure regulator.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: After correcting root cause, replace contaminated sensor.

faq.q: Should I replace the catalyst too?
faq.a: Only if a P0430 is also present or the catalyst is physically damaged.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
shortDescription: Bank 2 downstream O2 sensor is reacting too slowly.
fullDescription:
P0159 indicates the downstream O2 sensor on Bank 2 transitions too slowly when fuel trim changes. Since downstream sensors are also used to assess catalyst efficiency, slow response can trigger P0430 if left unaddressed.

It is almost always an aged or contaminated sensor.

fixSteps.title: Graph O2 response
fixSteps.instruction: Perform throttle snap and observe sensor reaction time.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new OE-quality O2 sensor.

faq.q: Do I need to replace both sensors?
faq.a: Not necessarily, but if one is aged the other likely is too.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Bank 2 Sensor 3 O2 signal is out of range or invalid.
fullDescription:
P0162 is set when the third O2 sensor on Bank 2, typically near the tailpipe, reports an invalid signal. It is seen on vehicles with secondary catalysts and three sensors per bank.

The fix is usually sensor replacement after confirming harness integrity.

fixSteps.title: Scan live data
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect sensor output compared to others.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new OE-quality O2 sensor.

faq.a: Farthest downstream on the bank opposite cylinder #1.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Bank 2 Sensor 3 signal voltage stays low.
fullDescription: P0163 sets when the third O2 sensor on Bank 2 is below ~0.1 V longer than allowed. Check upstream exhaust leaks and harness integrity; if both are good, replace the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Exhaust inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Check flanges, slip joints, and catalyst shells.

fixSteps.title: Wiring check
fixSteps.instruction: Measure signal for short to ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor.

faq.a: Yes — any active O2 code will cause a state inspection failure.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Bank 2 Sensor 3 voltage stuck high.
fullDescription: P0164 is set when Bank 2 Sensor 3 voltage remains above ~0.9 V for longer than allowed. Rich running, contamination, or signal wire shorted to voltage are typical causes.

fixSteps.title: Check fuel trims
fixSteps.instruction: Identify if engine is running rich.

fixSteps.title: Correct rich cause
fixSteps.instruction: Fix leaking injector / FPR issues.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor.

faq.a: Yes — oil coats the sensor and biases it rich.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Bank 2 Sensor 3 responds to mixture changes too slowly.
fullDescription: P0165 indicates an aging or contaminated Bank 2 Sensor 3 O2 sensor. Response time exceeds the calibration threshold during fuel trim transitions.

fixSteps.title: Graph response
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool to observe transition time.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new O2 sensor.

faq.a: Not urgent but will prevent emissions readiness from completing.

title: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
shortDescription: Bank 2 Sensor 3 shows no voltage activity.
fullDescription: P0166 indicates the ECM cannot detect activity from Bank 2 Sensor 3. The signal stays near bias voltage, typical of a dead sensor element or heater failure.

fixSteps.title: Check heater fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Verify heater fuse intact.

fixSteps.title: Wiring check
fixSteps.instruction: Verify continuity of signal and ground wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new O2 sensor.

faq.a: No — the O2 and catalyst monitors need an active sensor.

title: Fuel Trim Malfunction (Bank 2)
shortDescription: Bank 2 fuel trim is out of normal range.
fullDescription:
P0173 indicates the ECM cannot keep Bank 2 fuel trims within the programmed window. Either long-term trim is pegged positive (lean) or negative (rich).

Causes mirror P0170: vacuum leaks, fuel delivery, sensor drift. Always diagnose with live short-term and long-term trim values.
safetyImpact: Low to medium — driveability and possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Read fuel trims
fixSteps.instruction: Compare STFT and LTFT at idle and cruise.

fixSteps.title: Smoke test intake
fixSteps.instruction: Find vacuum leaks with smoke machine.

fixSteps.title: Test fuel pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Verify pressure at idle and snap throttle.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Spray with MAF cleaner only.

faq.q: Is P0173 the same as P0170?
faq.a: Same problem but different bank — P0173 is Bank 2.

title: Fuel Composition Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: Flex-fuel composition sensor reporting implausible values.
fullDescription:
P0177 is set on flex-fuel (FFV) vehicles when the fuel composition (alcohol/ethanol content) sensor signal is out of range or does not correlate with fuel trim learned values. Common on older GM and Ford FFV platforms.

Causes include a failed sensor, wiring fault, or contaminated fuel.
safetyImpact: Low — driveability may degrade on E85.

fixSteps.title: Scan sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: Check reported ethanol percentage.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Locate sensor (usually on fuel line near tank) and inspect wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new fuel composition sensor.

faq.q: Do I have to use E85?
faq.a: No — the vehicle runs fine on regular gasoline.

title: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: Fuel temperature sensor output does not correlate with engine conditions.
fullDescription:
P0181 is set when the fuel temperature sensor signal is out of expected range. This is more common on flex-fuel and diesel applications, where fuel temperature is used for density compensation.

Typical causes are sensor drift, wiring issues, or a fuel heater problem.

fixSteps.title: Scan live data
fixSteps.instruction: Check reported fuel temp vs ambient.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new fuel temperature sensor.

faq.a: Slightly — incorrect temp compensation skews fueling.

title: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low Input
shortDescription: Fuel temperature sensor signal voltage below minimum.
fullDescription: P0182 is set when the ECM reads the fuel temp sensor at or near 0 V. This usually means a short to ground in the signal wire, failed sensor, or connector corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Disconnect sensor
fixSteps.instruction: With sensor unplugged, signal wire should read ~5 V.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Measure signal wire for short to ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new fuel temp sensor if wiring is good.

faq.a: No, but it should be diagnosed promptly.

title: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: Fuel rail pressure sensor signal does not match expected values.
fullDescription:
P0191 is set when the fuel rail pressure sensor signal does not correlate with commanded pressure. The code is most common on direct injection gas (GDI) and diesel common-rail engines.

Causes include a failing sensor, restricted fuel supply, leaking injectors, or worn high-pressure pump.
safetyImpact: Medium — can cause stalling, no-start, or limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Compare commanded vs actual pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Scan tool live data during cranking and idle.

fixSteps.title: Replace fuel filter
fixSteps.instruction: Replace if due.

fixSteps.title: Test HP fuel pump
fixSteps.instruction: Measure supply pressure to rail.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new rail pressure sensor if testing isolates it.

faq.a: No — drive to a shop; internal damage is possible if you ignore this.

title: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Low Input
shortDescription: Fuel rail pressure sensor signal stays at low voltage.
fullDescription:
P0192 is set when the fuel rail pressure sensor voltage is below the expected range. Root causes include a failed sensor, open reference voltage, or a signal wire shorted to ground.

On GDI engines this often triggers limp mode because fuel pressure control is critical.

fixSteps.title: Check connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect for bent pins and water intrusion.

fixSteps.title: Verify 5 V reference
fixSteps.instruction: Should be close to 5 V key on.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new rail pressure sensor to spec torque.

faq.q: Can I reset the code and drive?
faq.a: It will return immediately. Proper repair is required.

title: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit High Input
shortDescription: Fuel rail pressure sensor voltage is above the allowed maximum.
fullDescription:
P0193 is set when the fuel rail pressure sensor signal exceeds the upper voltage threshold. This can indicate a genuinely over-pressurized rail, a shorted signal wire, or an open ground to the sensor.

Driveability is often severely affected; limp mode is common on GDI and diesel engines.
safetyImpact: Medium — limp mode or stalling possible.

fixSteps.title: Verify ground
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe sensor ground wire.

fixSteps.title: Check pressure control
fixSteps.instruction: Read actual rail pressure vs commanded.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If wiring is good and pressure reads normal mechanically, replace.

faq.q: Can a stuck regulator set P0193?
faq.a: Yes — true over-pressure conditions will.

title: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent
shortDescription: Fuel rail pressure sensor signal is intermittent or noisy.
fullDescription:
P0194 indicates the ECM sees intermittent drops or spikes in the rail pressure sensor signal. Almost always caused by a loose connector, chafed harness, or failing sensor element.

These can be hard to reproduce; wiggle-testing the harness during live data capture often finds the fault.

fixSteps.title: Wiggle test harness
fixSteps.instruction: Capture live data while moving harness.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Repair chafes or replace connector pins.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If wiring good, replace the sensor.

faq.q: Can I ignore it if it's intermittent?
faq.a: No — intermittent fuel pressure faults can strand you unexpectedly.

title: Injector Circuit Malfunction - Cylinder 9
shortDescription: Electrical fault on cylinder 9 fuel injector circuit.
fullDescription:
P0209 is set on V10 engines when the ECM detects an electrical problem on the cylinder 9 injector circuit. It is essentially a duplicate of P0201-P0208 but targeted at the 9th cylinder.

Causes include an open or shorted injector, damaged wiring, or a failed ECM driver.

fixSteps.title: Measure injector resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Compare to other injectors (should match within 1 ohm).

fixSteps.title: Check harness
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect injector wiring and connector.

fixSteps.title: Swap injector
fixSteps.instruction: Swap with a known-good cylinder to confirm.

faq.a: Dodge Viper, Ford V10 trucks, and some Audi/Lamborghini V10s.

title: Injector Circuit Malfunction - Cylinder 10
shortDescription: Electrical fault on cylinder 10 fuel injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0210 covers cylinder 10 injector circuit faults on V10 engines. Diagnosis is identical to other injector circuit codes: check resistance, wiring, and ECM driver.

fixSteps.title: Test resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Compare injector ohms to other cylinders.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check harness and connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injector if testing confirms.

faq.q: Can I drive with this code?
faq.a: Not recommended — misfire can damage the catalyst.

title: Injector Circuit Malfunction - Cylinder 11
shortDescription: Electrical fault on cylinder 11 fuel injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0211 applies to V12 engines. The ECM detects an injector circuit fault on cylinder 11. Typical diagnosis workflow mirrors other injector codes.

fixSteps.title: Resistance test
fixSteps.instruction: Check injector coil resistance.

fixSteps.title: Harness test
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm continuity from ECM to injector.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new OE injector.

faq.a: Yes — OE parts are specialty and often costly.

title: Injector Circuit Malfunction - Cylinder 12
shortDescription: Electrical fault on cylinder 12 fuel injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0212 is the V12-specific code for cylinder 12 injector circuit problems. Similar diagnosis to other injector codes.

fixSteps.title: Resistance check
fixSteps.instruction: Compare to other injectors.

fixSteps.title: Wiring inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect for chafed wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new OE injector.

faq.q: Is V12 harder to work on?
faq.a: Yes — access on 12-cylinder engines is limited; shop service often preferred.

title: Cold Start Injector 1 Malfunction
shortDescription: Electrical or performance fault on the first cold start injector.
fullDescription:
P0213 is set on older vehicles (primarily 1990s Toyota/Lexus and some European models) equipped with a supplemental cold-start injector. The ECM detects a fault in circuit 1.

Repair involves checking the dedicated wiring, thermo-time switch (where fitted), and the injector itself.

fixSteps.title: Check injector
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance and check for spray.

fixSteps.title: Test thermo switch
fixSteps.instruction: Verify switch closes below spec temperature.

fixSteps.title: Replace component
fixSteps.instruction: Install new cold start injector or thermo switch.

faq.q: Does my modern car have this?
faq.a: No — cold-start injectors are obsolete; modern engines use GDI or pulse widening.

title: Cold Start Injector 2 Malfunction
shortDescription: Electrical or performance fault on the second cold start injector.
fullDescription: P0214 is similar to P0213 but pertains to the second cold start injector, found on some larger-displacement engines.

fixSteps.title: Check injector
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance and inspect spray.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injector.

faq.a: Some large V8/V12 imports had two; not common today.

title: Engine Shutoff Solenoid Malfunction
shortDescription: Electrical fault in the engine fuel shutoff solenoid circuit.
fullDescription:
P0215 indicates a malfunction in the fuel shutoff solenoid, which in older diesels is responsible for cutting fuel to the injection pump when the ignition is off. A failure can prevent starting or cause run-on at shutdown.

Common on 1990s–2000s diesels with mechanical rotary pumps.
safetyImpact: Medium — engine may fail to start or refuse to stop.

fixSteps.title: Check fuse and power
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power to solenoid with key on.

fixSteps.title: Test solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for click when 12 V applied.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Install new shutoff solenoid.

faq.a: Primarily diesel engines with mechanical injection pumps.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor C Circuit High
shortDescription: Third TPS/APP channel voltage above allowed maximum.
fullDescription:
P0228 is set on drive-by-wire systems that use three redundant sensors on the throttle pedal. The 'C' channel signal is reading higher than expected, which the ECM interprets as a mismatched or failed sensor.

Repair usually involves replacing the accelerator pedal position sensor assembly.
safetyImpact: High — may trigger limp mode or reduced power.

fixSteps.title: Scan APP values
fixSteps.instruction: Compare APP1/2/3 percentages through full pedal travel.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect for chafed wires at pedal assembly.

fixSteps.title: Replace pedal assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Install new APP sensor / pedal assembly.

faq.a: Yes — once the code is fixed and cleared.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor C Circuit Intermittent
shortDescription: Intermittent signal from the third TPS/APP channel.
fullDescription: P0229 indicates an intermittent fault on the APP/TPS channel C. Common on cars with loose pedal assembly bolts or chafed harnesses where the pedal pivots.
safetyImpact: High — can trigger sudden reduced power.

fixSteps.title: Wiggle test connector
fixSteps.instruction: Live data while moving pedal harness.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Repair chafes or reseat connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace pedal assembly
fixSteps.instruction: If intermittent persists, install new APP.

faq.a: Not recommended — sudden power loss is unpredictable.

title: Fuel Pump Secondary Circuit Intermittent
shortDescription: Intermittent electrical fault on the secondary fuel pump circuit.
fullDescription:
P0233 is set when the ECM or fuel pump driver module detects an intermittent open or short in the pump circuit. Typical on vehicles with returnless fuel systems and pump driver modules.

Common causes include corroded pump connectors and failing driver modules.
safetyImpact: High — may cause stalling and no-start.

fixSteps.title: Inspect pump connector
fixSteps.instruction: Remove rear seat access and inspect above tank.

fixSteps.title: Test driver module
fixSteps.instruction: Scan tool may show PWM duty cycle anomalies.

fixSteps.title: Replace module/pump
fixSteps.instruction: Replace whichever tests bad.

faq.a: A proper weather-pack repair kit works; avoid electrical tape fixes.

title: Turbo/Super Charger Overboost Condition
shortDescription: Turbocharger or supercharger boost pressure exceeds maximum.
fullDescription:
P0234 is set when the intake boost pressure exceeds the ECM's upper threshold. Causes include a stuck wastegate, failed boost control solenoid, or aftermarket tuning.

Overboost can damage pistons, head gaskets, and the turbo itself — address promptly.
safetyImpact: High — severe overboost can destroy the engine.

fixSteps.title: Scan live boost data
fixSteps.instruction: Note actual vs commanded boost.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wastegate
fixSteps.instruction: Verify actuator moves freely.

fixSteps.title: Test boost solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Verify proper PWM operation.

fixSteps.title: Repair/replace
fixSteps.instruction: Replace solenoid or repair wastegate linkage.

faq.a: No — overboost is an engine-killing condition. Limp home and repair.

title: Cylinder 5 Injector Circuit Low
shortDescription: Low signal on cylinder 5 injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0273 indicates the ECM detected low voltage on cylinder 5's injector circuit. Usually points to an open injector coil, open wiring, or failed driver.

fixSteps.title: Test injector
fixSteps.instruction: Measure coil resistance.

fixSteps.title: Check harness
fixSteps.instruction: Verify continuity from ECM to injector.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injector if coil is open.

faq.q: Can low fuel pressure set this?
faq.a: No — P0273 is an electrical circuit code.

title: Cylinder 5 Injector Circuit High
shortDescription: High signal on cylinder 5 injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0274 indicates the injector driver sees a high voltage condition on cylinder 5's circuit. Typical causes are a short to voltage or a shorted injector coil.

fixSteps.title: Measure resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Compare to other injectors.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Look for pinched or melted wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injector.

faq.a: Yes — repeated shorts can damage injector drivers.

title: Cylinder 6 Injector Circuit Low
shortDescription: Low signal on cylinder 6 injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0280 is set when the ECM reads low voltage on the cylinder 6 injector driver output. Usually caused by an open injector, broken wire, or failed driver.

fixSteps.title: Test injector
fixSteps.instruction: Measure coil resistance.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Continuity test harness.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injector if coil open.

faq.q: Do I replace all six injectors?
faq.a: Only the failed one, unless others test marginal.

title: Cylinder 6 Injector Circuit High
shortDescription: High signal on cylinder 6 injector circuit.
fullDescription: P0281 indicates the injector driver sees elevated voltage on cylinder 6's circuit, typical of a shorted coil or wiring short to power.

fixSteps.title: Resistance test
fixSteps.instruction: Compare to other injectors.

fixSteps.title: Wiring check
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect for chafes and shorts.

fixSteps.title: Replace injector
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injector.

faq.a: No — this is an electrical fault, not a flow issue.

title: Turbo/Super Charger Underboost Condition
shortDescription: Turbocharger or supercharger is producing less boost than commanded.
fullDescription:
P0299 is one of the most common turbo-related codes. It is set when measured boost falls significantly below commanded boost during acceleration. Causes include boost leaks, failed wastegate actuator, worn turbo, or a dirty MAF.

A smoke test of the intake tract is the fastest way to find boost leaks.

fixSteps.title: Scan live boost
fixSteps.instruction: Compare commanded vs actual boost.

fixSteps.title: Smoke test intake
fixSteps.instruction: Pressurize intake to find leaks.

fixSteps.title: Check wastegate
fixSteps.instruction: Verify actuator movement at full boost.

fixSteps.title: Repair leaks / replace parts
fixSteps.instruction: Replace cracked hoses, failed solenoid, or turbo.

faq.q: Can a dirty air filter cause P0299?
faq.a: Severely clogged filters can — check filter first, it's free.

faq.q: Is the turbo always the problem?
faq.a: No — boost leaks are the most common cause by far.

title: Cylinder 9 Misfire Detected
shortDescription: Misfire detected on cylinder 9.
fullDescription:
P0309 indicates the ECM has detected a misfire on cylinder 9 (V10 engines). Causes include a bad ignition coil, spark plug, injector, or compression problem.

Start with inexpensive items — swap coil and plug from a working cylinder to see if the misfire follows.

fixSteps.title: Swap coil and plug
fixSteps.instruction: Move parts from cylinder 9 to another; see if misfire follows.

fixSteps.title: Compression test
fixSteps.instruction: If misfire stays, compression test cylinder 9.

fixSteps.title: Replace failed component
fixSteps.instruction: Install new coil/plug/injector as needed.

faq.q: Should I drive with flashing MIL?
faq.a: No — a flashing MIL indicates active misfire that damages the catalytic converter.

title: Cylinder 10 Misfire Detected
shortDescription: Misfire detected on cylinder 10.
fullDescription: P0310 indicates a misfire on cylinder 10 of a V10 engine. Diagnosis mirrors other cylinder-specific misfire codes.

fixSteps.title: Swap ignition parts
fixSteps.instruction: Move coil/plug to another cylinder.

fixSteps.title: Compression test
fixSteps.instruction: Check if mechanical.

fixSteps.title: Replace part
fixSteps.instruction: Install new failing component.

faq.a: Only on V10 vehicles — relatively rare.

title: Crankshaft Position System Variation Not Learned
shortDescription: The ECM has not completed the crankshaft variation learn procedure.
fullDescription:
P0315 is set when the ECM has not stored the crankshaft reluctor wheel variation values. This learn procedure compensates for manufacturing tolerances in the crank reluctor and is required for accurate misfire detection.

Commonly appears after crank sensor or ECM replacement. Fix is performing the CKP variation relearn with a capable scan tool.

causes.cause: ECM or crank sensor recently replaced

fixSteps.title: Perform CKP relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Follow OEM procedure with capable scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Clear codes
fixSteps.instruction: Clear and verify no return.

faq.q: Do I need a dealer for the relearn?
faq.a: No — many aftermarket bidirectional scan tools support CKP variation relearn.

title: Engine Misfire Detected on Startup (First 1000 Revs)
shortDescription: Misfire detected within the first 1000 engine revolutions after startup.
fullDescription:
P0316 is set when the ECM detects misfires during the initial crank/startup period. Ford uses this code commonly on diesel PowerStroke engines and some gas engines, to catch cold-start specific misfires.

Causes include fuel injector problems, low compression, or glow plug issues (diesel).
symptoms: Puff of white smoke at startup

fixSteps.title: Pull misfire counters
fixSteps.instruction: Identify which cylinders misfire at startup.

fixSteps.title: Test glow plugs / injectors
fixSteps.instruction: Check relevant component per fuel type.

fixSteps.title: Replace component
fixSteps.instruction: Install new injectors or glow plugs.

faq.a: Yes — P0316 specifically catches cold-start misfires.

title: Ignition/Distributor Engine Speed Input Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in the engine speed input signal to the ECM.
fullDescription:
P0320 is set when the ECM detects a fault in the engine speed (RPM) signal from the distributor, crank, or cam sensor. The engine may stall or refuse to start.

Causes include a failed sensor, wiring damage, or loose reluctor wheel.

fixSteps.title: Scan data
fixSteps.instruction: Watch RPM signal while cranking.

fixSteps.title: Test sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Check resistance and signal output.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor.

faq.a: Not recommended — stalling risk is high.

title: Ignition/Distributor Engine Speed Input Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: Engine speed signal present but out of range.
fullDescription: P0321 sets when the engine speed signal exists but jumps around or does not correlate with engine load. Often caused by a loose reluctor wheel, damaged tone ring, or failing sensor.

fixSteps.title: Scope the signal
fixSteps.instruction: Use scope to view sensor waveform for dropouts.

fixSteps.title: Inspect tone ring
fixSteps.instruction: Check for damaged teeth or debris.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If waveform is good, replace sensor.

faq.a: A weak starter with slow cranking can trigger it briefly.

title: Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1)
shortDescription: Knock sensor 1 signal is out of expected range.
fullDescription:
P0326 indicates the Bank 1 knock sensor signal amplitude is abnormally high or low during expected operating conditions. The ECM cannot use it to adapt ignition timing effectively, so timing is retarded for safety.

Causes include a failing sensor, loose mounting bolt, or a drifted sensor element.
safetyImpact: Low — the engine runs on a conservative timing map.

fixSteps.title: Tighten sensor bolt
fixSteps.instruction: Torque knock sensor to specification.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check shielded cable for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor.

faq.a: On some engines it's under the intake manifold — quite labor intensive.

title: Knock Sensor 2 Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
shortDescription: Knock sensor 2 (Bank 2) signal is out of expected range.
fullDescription: P0331 is the Bank 2 equivalent of P0326. Diagnosis is the same: check mounting, wiring, and replace the sensor if output is abnormal.

fixSteps.title: Retorque sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Torque to spec.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new knock sensor.

faq.a: No — this is an electrical fault, not a fuel octane issue.

title: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Low Input
shortDescription: Crankshaft position sensor voltage is below the expected minimum.
fullDescription:
P0337 is set when the CKP sensor signal stays low. Typical causes are a failed sensor, shorted signal wire, or a damaged reluctor ring.

Cranks-no-starts are possible when the signal fails entirely.

fixSteps.title: Scope signal
fixSteps.instruction: View waveform during cranking.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check for shorts.

fixSteps.title: Replace CKP
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor.

faq.a: Yes — many CKP sensors fail when hot and recover when cool.

title: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit High Input
shortDescription: Crankshaft position sensor voltage exceeds the maximum.
fullDescription:
P0338 is set when the CKP signal stays near its upper voltage limit. Causes include a failed sensor, open sensor ground, or signal shorted to voltage.

Diagnose with an oscilloscope for best accuracy.

fixSteps.title: Verify ground
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe CKP ground.

fixSteps.title: Scope signal
fixSteps.instruction: Check waveform.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new CKP.

faq.q: How long does CKP replacement take?
faq.a: Typically 1-2 hours depending on engine access.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Low Input (Bank 1)
shortDescription: Cam position sensor signal stays low.
fullDescription:
P0342 is set when the Bank 1 camshaft position sensor voltage reads below the minimum threshold. Likely causes include a failed sensor, shorted signal wire, or a damaged cam reluctor.

Expect extended cranking or no-start in severe cases.

fixSteps.title: Scope signal
fixSteps.instruction: Observe cam sensor waveform.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check for shorts.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new cam sensor.

faq.a: Usually yes — modern engines can start on CKP alone.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit High Input (Bank 1)
shortDescription: Cam position sensor voltage stays high.
fullDescription: P0343 is set when the Bank 1 CMP sensor signal exceeds the maximum voltage. Causes mirror P0342 but on the high side — failed sensor, open ground, or short to voltage.

fixSteps.title: Verify ground
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe the CMP ground.

fixSteps.title: Scope signal
fixSteps.instruction: Check waveform.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor.

faq.a: Yes — variable valve timing requires a good CMP signal.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1)
shortDescription: Intermittent Bank 1 cam sensor signal.
fullDescription: P0344 indicates intermittent loss of the Bank 1 cam sensor signal. Commonly caused by a loose connector, chafed harness, or a sensor that fails when hot.

fixSteps.title: Wiggle test
fixSteps.instruction: Scan data while moving harness and connector.

fixSteps.title: Reseat connector
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure connector fully engages.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If intermittent persists, replace sensor.

faq.a: Yes short term, but intermittent stalls can strand you.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
shortDescription: Bank 2 cam sensor signal is out of expected range.
fullDescription: P0346 is set when the Bank 2 camshaft position sensor signal is implausible in timing or pattern relative to CKP. Causes are similar to P0341: reluctor damage, stretched timing chain, or sensor drift.

fixSteps.title: Read VVT data
fixSteps.instruction: Check commanded vs actual cam advance.

fixSteps.title: Oil level check
fixSteps.instruction: Low oil can affect phaser operation.

fixSteps.title: Inspect timing chain
fixSteps.instruction: Check for chain stretch; inspect tensioner.

fixSteps.title: Replace component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace sensor, phaser, or chain as needed.

faq.q: Could it just be oil change overdue?
faq.a: Yes — low or dirty oil affects cam phasers; change oil first.

title: Crankshaft Position Sensor B Circuit
shortDescription: Electrical fault in the secondary crankshaft position sensor circuit.
fullDescription:
P0385 applies to vehicles with a secondary CKP sensor (often on the flywheel/flex-plate). It is set when the ECM detects a fault in that circuit.

Causes include sensor failure, harness damage, or tone ring damage.

fixSteps.title: Scope signal
fixSteps.instruction: Check CKP B waveform while cranking.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check harness integrity.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new secondary CKP.

faq.a: Some diesels and high-end performance engines for redundancy.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit (Bank 2)
shortDescription: Electrical fault in Bank 2 Cam Sensor B circuit.
fullDescription:
P0390 applies to engines with dual overhead cams on Bank 2, where the exhaust camshaft has its own sensor. The ECM has detected an electrical fault in that sensor's circuit.

Causes are typical for sensor codes: failed sensor, wiring damage, or connector corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Inspect connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check for bent pins or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Scope signal
fixSteps.instruction: Verify waveform.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new cam sensor.

faq.a: Mildly — VVT optimization is compromised.
